1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |

63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |

68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}

49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}

171	  %  $$

203	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
204	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
206	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
207	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
208	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
209	  % L4220-L4228).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.

365	[Localization criterion for module sheafification]

371	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
372	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
373	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
374	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).

492	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
493	    \qquad
494	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto (s \cdot m) \otimes n,
495	  \]
496	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
497	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{P}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
498	  action map \(\mathrm{actLmap}\) of
499	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}; together with the parallel
500	  right-action transformation it is one of the two rows whose coequalizer
501	  presents the relative tensor presheaf \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
502	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}). Naturality in \(U\) is the
503	  compatibility of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction
504	  of \(s \cdot m\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), so \(a_L\) commutes with the
505	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps; this is checked on
506	  elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
507	\end{definition}

509	\begin{lemma}[A \(\mathbb{Z}\)-whiskered stalkwise isomorphism is a local isomorphism]
510	  \label{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}
511	  % NOTE: Lean decl name pending --- no `\lean{}` pin yet; add when the Lean
512	  % declaration for this whiskered-stalkwise-iso lemma lands.
513	  \uses{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
514	  Let \(f : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be a morphism of abelian-group
515	  presheaves on the space \(X\) that is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism} (each
516	  stalk map \(f_x\) is an isomorphism), and let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a further
517	  abelian-group presheaf. Then the whiskered map
518	  \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}\) of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
519	  presheaves is again a stalkwise isomorphism, hence a local isomorphism (a
520	  member of \(J.W\) for the opens topology \(J\)).
521	\end{lemma}
522	
523	\begin{proof}
524	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
525	  The stalk functor \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \mathcal{A}_x\) at a point \(x\) is a
526	  filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods of \(x\), and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
527	  commutes with filtered colimits, so
528	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C})_x \cong
529	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C}_x\) naturally in \(\mathcal{A}\).
530	  Under this identification the stalk of \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is
531	  \(f_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism since \(f_x\) is.
532	  Thus \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is a stalkwise isomorphism; a
533	  stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves on a space is exactly a local
534	  isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\).
535	\end{proof}

542	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more

554	\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]
555	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
556	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
557	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
558	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
559	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
560	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
561	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
562	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
563	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
564	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
565	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
566	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
567	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
568	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
569	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
570	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
571	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
572	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac,
573	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap_tmul,
574	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap_tmul,
575	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_tmul,
576	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_apply,
577	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom_tmul}
578	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
579	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
580	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
581	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
582	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
583	  \[
584	    a_L,\, a_R :
585	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
586	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
587	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
588	  \]
589	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
590	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
591	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
592	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
593	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
594	\end{lemma}
595	
596	\begin{proof}
597	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
598	  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
599	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
600	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
601	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
602	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
603	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
604	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
605	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
606	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
607	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
608	
609	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
610	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
611	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
612	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
613	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
614	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
615	
616	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
617	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
618	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
619	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
620	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
621	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
622	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
623	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
624	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
625	  colimit cofork.
626	
627	\end{proof}

629	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
630	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
631	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
632	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
633	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
634	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
635	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
636	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
637	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
638	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
639	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
640	  parallel pair
641	  \[
642	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
643	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
644	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
645	  \]
646	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
647	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
648	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
649	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
650	\end{lemma}
651	
652	\begin{proof}
653	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
654	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
655	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
656	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
657	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
658	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
659	  three steps.
660	
661	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
662	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
663	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
664	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
665	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
666	  \[
667	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
668	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
669	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
670	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
671	  \]
672	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
673	
674	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families assemble into
675	  morphisms of abelian-group presheaves
676	  \[
677	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
678	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
679	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
680	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
681	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
682	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
683	  \]
684	  i.e.\ natural transformations of functors
685	  \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\). Naturality of
686	  \(a_L, a_R, \pi\) in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the
687	  restriction maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\): the restriction
688	  of \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection,
689	  because the relative tensor is functorial in \(U\). Colimits in a functor
690	  category are computed objectwise --- by
691	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
692	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
693	  coequalizers of step 1 assemble into a coequalizer of the displayed parallel
694	  pair in \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\).
695	
696	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
697	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
698	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
699	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
700	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
701	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
702	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
703	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
704	  along this identification exhibits
705	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
706	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\).
707	\end{proof}

742	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
743	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
744	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
745	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
746	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
747	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
748	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
749	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
750	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
751	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
752	  the sheafification adjunction
753	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
754	  presheaves of modules, and write
755	  \[
756	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
757	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
758	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
759	  \]
760	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
761	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
762	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
763	  \[
764	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
765	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
766	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
767	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
768	  \]
769	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
770	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
771	\end{lemma}
772	
773	\begin{proof}
774	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
775	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
776	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
777	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
778	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
779	    lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
780	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
781	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
782	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
783	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
784	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
785	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
786	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
787	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
788	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
789	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
790	
791	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
792	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
793	  is the objectwise relative tensor
794	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
795	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
796	  maps
797	  \[
798	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
799	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
800	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
801	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
802	  \]
803	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
804	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
805	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
806	  pair
807	  \[
808	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
809	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
810	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
811	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
812	  \]
813	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
814	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
815	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
816	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
817	
818	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
819	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
820	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
821	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
822	  of \(a\)-images. It remains to see that the two whiskered comparison maps
823	  relating the rows,
824	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) and
825	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
826	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), lie in \(J.W\) (equivalently, become
827	  isomorphisms after applying \(a\)).
828	
829	  \textbf{Whiskered units are stalkwise isomorphisms.} We argue this via stalks
830	  of abelian-group presheaves (\(\mathrm{TopCat.Presheaf.stalk}\), which exist
831	  for abelian presheaves on the underlying space, unlike module-sheaf stalks).
832	  The sheafification unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to a\mathcal{P}\)
833	  is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism}: for each point \(x\) the stalk map
834	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x : \mathcal{P}_x \to (a\mathcal{P})_x\) is an
835	  isomorphism (the defining property of the associated-sheaf unit). The stalk of
836	  a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of stalks,
837	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B})_x \cong
838	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B}_x\), because the stalk is a
839	  filtered colimit and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) commutes with filtered colimits.
840	  Under this identification the stalk of
841	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) at \(x\) is
842	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism
843	  (tensoring an isomorphism with the identity). Hence each whiskered unit is a
844	  stalkwise isomorphism, and a stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves
845	  on a topological space is a local isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\)
846	  (\cref{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}). Applying \(a\) therefore turns
847	  both whiskered comparison maps into isomorphisms.
848	
849	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
850	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
851	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
852	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
853	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
854	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
855	\end{proof}

1053	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
1054	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
1055	
1056	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
1057	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
1058	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
1059	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
1060	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
1061	global sections.
1062	
1063	\begin{definition}\leanok
1064	[Section multiplication]
1065	  \label{def:sectionMul}
1066	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
1067	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1068	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1069	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
1070	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
1071	  \[
1072	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
1073	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
1074	    \;\longrightarrow\;
1075	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
1076	  \]
1077	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
1078	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
1079	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
1080	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
1081	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
1082	  the sheafification unit
1083	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
1084	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
1085	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
1086	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
1087	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
1088	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
1089	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
1090	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
1091	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
1092	\end{definition}
1093	
1094	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
1095	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
1096	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
1097	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1098	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
1099	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
1100	  the two composites
1101	  \[
1102	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
1103	    \longrightarrow
1104	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
1105	    \cong
1106	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
1107	  \]
1108	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
1109	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
1110	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
1111	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
1112	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
1113	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).

1118	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1119	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
1120	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
1121	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
1122	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
1123	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
1124	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
1125	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
1126	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
1127	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
1128	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
1129	  section multiplications.
1130	\end{proof}

1133	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}

1135	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1136	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1137	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1138	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1139	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.

1141	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1142	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1143	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
1144	  \mathlibok
1145	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1146	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1147	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1148	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1149	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1150	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1151	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
1152	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of

1157	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
